planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; lavandin does not come true from seed and must be propagated vegetatively. It is more vigorous and faster-growing than English lavender. Grosso is the most widely planted cultivar for oil production and dried bundles; Provence and Fred Boutin are also excellent landscape cultivars.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; lavandin is a naturally occurring and cultivated hybrid between English lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) and spike lavender (L. latifolia) that has become the dominant commercial lavender worldwide — over 90 percent of the lavender essential oil sold globally comes from lavandin rather than true English lavender. It is larger, more vigorous, and more heat- and drought-tolerant than English lavender, with longer, more branched flower spikes and a stronger scent that is higher in camphor and has a slightly sharper, more penetrating quality than the pure English lavender fragrance. As a sterile hybrid, it produces no viable seed but blooms heavily and reliably. The cultivar Grosso produces the most oil and the largest, most dramatic dried bundles; Provence is the classic lavender-field cultivar of the Provence region of France. Excellent for mass planting, dried flower production, cut flowers, and herb gardens. Slightly less cold-hardy than English lavender but more heat-tolerant in zones 7-8.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; French lavender is less cold-hardy than English lavender and is damaged below 25 degrees F, making it suitable for zones 8-11 or as an annual in colder climates. In mild climates it blooms nearly year-round. Trim after each flush of bloom to keep compact and prevent legginess.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; French lavender is a distinctly different lavender species from English lavender, immediately recognizable by its linear, gray-green leaves with serrated, tooth-like margins along their length — the dentata name refers directly to these toothed leaf edges. The oblong flower spikes are topped with small, pale-purple, feathery bracts and produce a strong, somewhat medicinal-aromatic scent. In zones 8-11, it blooms nearly year-round — producing flowers in almost every month — making it by far the longest-blooming lavender species and an invaluable year-round pollinator plant in mild climates. Native to rocky Mediterranean hillsides and the Canary Islands, it thrives in the heat, drought, and alkaline soils of Gulf Coast and California gardens where English lavender can struggle with summer humidity. An excellent choice for low hedges, herb gardens, and companion planting in drought-tolerant borders where a near-perpetual lavender bloom is desired.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or summer in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; lemon thyme does not come true from seed and must be propagated vegetatively to maintain the lemon fragrance. Cultivars include Aureus (gold-variegated leaves) and Silver Queen (cream-variegated leaves), both ornamental and culinary. Trim after flowering to prevent woodiness.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; lemon thyme provides the same low-growing, woody-stemmed, small-leafed thyme habit as common thyme but with distinctly and strongly lemon-scented foliage from the volatile oil constituents geraniol and linalool. The lemon fragrance is immediately apparent on touching the leaves and makes lemon thyme particularly valuable for fish dishes, roasted poultry, herb butters, cream sauces, and lemon-scented baked goods where a citrus-thyme combination is desired. Pale-pink to lavender-pink flowers in early summer attract bees and other pollinators. The variegated cultivars Aureus (golden-edged leaves) and Silver Queen (cream-variegated) add bright foliage color to the herb garden edge, rock garden, or container planting while retaining the full lemon-thyme culinary flavor. Grows identically to common thyme in its requirements for very well-drained, lean soil and full sun exposure.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or divide established plants; grow in sharply drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun. Named cultivars including Herrenhausen, Hopley's Purple, and Rosenkuppel are consistently more floriferous than seedlings and must be propagated by cuttings or division to maintain flower character. Dead-head or cut back after the first bloom flush to encourage re-blooming through fall.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; ornamental oregano is an outstanding long-season pollinator plant from rocky hillsides of Turkey and Cyprus, producing masses of tiny tubular purple-pink flowers on wiry, branching stems from June through October — a bloom period of four to five months that makes it one of the longest-flowering hardy perennials available. The numerous small flowers are irresistible to bees, butterflies, and hoverflies throughout the long season. The foliage is aromatic like culinary oregano but the plant is grown primarily as an ornamental rather than for cooking, since the essential oil content is lower than culinary Greek or Italian oregano. The cultivar Herrenhausen is the most widely available and produces dense, upright clusters of deep rose-purple flowers on burgundy-flushed stems; Hopley's Purple is taller with more open, airy clusters. The dried seed heads and wiry stems remain attractive through winter and make excellent filler in dried arrangements. Excellent for the front of a dry border, Mediterranean-style plantings, and rock gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; prostrate rosemary is less cold-hardy than upright rosemary and is best suited to zones 8-11. It is an excellent groundcover on sunny slopes and tolerates salt spray, making it particularly valuable in coastal California and Gulf Coast gardens where it grows as a reliable evergreen groundcover.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; prostrate rosemary is the trailing, spreading form of rosemary that grows horizontally rather than upright, producing long, cascading stems that hug the ground or spill dramatically over walls, retaining walls, raised beds, and the lips of containers. The culinary use, flavor, and aromatic quality are identical to upright rosemary, but the growth habit is fundamentally different — prostrate rosemary reaches 1-2 feet tall and spreads 3-6 feet wide in a flowing groundcover form, making it an outstanding choice for erosion control on sunny slopes, as a cascading element in large containers, or as a low-water groundcover between stepping stones. Flowers are typically a deeper blue-violet than many upright forms and are produced freely from fall through spring in mild climates, providing nectar for honeybees and native bees during the cooler months. The salt tolerance makes it particularly valuable in coastal landscapes where few other herbs thrive.
